title: ## CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR CANNED FRUIT AND VEGETABLE PRODUCTS (CXC 2-1969)

sections.section: ## CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR CANNED FRUIT AND VEGETABLE PRODUCTS (CXC 2-1969)
sections.summary: Document title: Code of Hygienic Practice for Canned Fruit and Vegetable Products (CXC 2-1969).
Applies to fruit and vegetable products packed in hermetically sealed containers and processed by heat either before or after filling.

Defines key terms: 'hermetically sealed' as air‑tight; 'container' as any hermetic enclosure (e.g., metal, glass, laminated plastics); and 'heat processed' as heat treatment producing a safe product that will not spoil under normal non‑refrigerated storage and transport.

Sets raw material requirements covering environmental sanitation (safe disposal of wastes, sanitary irrigation water, and pest/disease control per appropriate official recommendations), sanitary harvesting/production (clean equipment and containers, sanitary techniques, segregation/removal of unfit materials, and protection from contamination), and transportation (cleanable conveyances and handling to prevent contamination and spoilage, using sanitary ice where applicable).

Conveyances must be adequate, constructed of cleanable materials and maintained to avoid contaminating the harvested crop or raw product, and handling procedures must prevent contamination or spoilage with refrigeration or sanitary ice used as indicated.

sections.section: SECTION IV - PLANT FACILITIES AND OPERATING REQUIREMENTS
sections.summary: Specifies plant facility and operating requirements including sanitary building design and layout, separation of processes, potable water and ice standards, plumbing and waste disposal approvals, lighting and ventilation, toilet and hand‑washing facilities, sanitary equipment materials/design, hygienic maintenance and vermin control, personnel health and hygiene, toxic substance control, raw material handling, inspection, washing, packaging, and heat processing under competent supervision with processing records for inspection.

Requires buildings and surrounding areas to be cleanable, of adequate size and sound construction to prevent pest ingress and allow cleaning, and details sanitary facilities including separation of processes, potable water and ice requirements, separate auxiliary water lines, approved plumbing and waste disposal, continuous waste removal, adequate lighting/ventilation, toilets with hand‑washing facilities, and accessible hand‑washing stations.

Specifies sanitary controls: separation of raw material and final product/inedible areas; potable water supply and potable ice requirements; separation of non‑potable auxiliary lines with no cross‑connections; watertight plumbing and approved waste disposal; continuous removal of solid/semi‑solid wastes; adequate lighting and ventilation with protected fixtures; sanitary toilets that do not open into food areas with associated hand‑washing facilities; and accessible hand‑washing stations with approved drying methods.

Areas where raw materials are received or stored must be physically separated from areas of final product preparation or packaging and from inedible material areas, and food handling areas must be completely separated from living quarters to preclude contamination.
Requires an ample supply of hot and cold water of potable quality, with potability standards at least those in WHO’s 1971 International Standards for Drinking Water.
Mandates that ice be made from potable water and manufactured, handled, stored and used in ways that protect it from contamination.

Permits non-potable auxiliary water (e.g., for fire control) only in completely separate, clearly identified lines (preferably colour-coded) with no cross-connections or back-siphonage to potable lines.

Requires plumbing and waste disposal lines to be sized for peak loads, watertight, and fitted with adequate traps and vents, with waste disposal conducted to avoid contamination of potable supplies and approved by the official agency having jurisdiction.

sections.section: (f) Removal of solid or semi-solid wastes from the product preparation and canning areas should be on a continuous or near continuous basis using water and/or appropriate equipment so that
sections.summary: Solid and semi-solid wastes from product preparation and canning areas must be removed continuously or near-continuously using water and/or appropriate equipment so areas stay clean, wastes are disposed so they cannot be used for human food, and disposal prevents contamination of food/water or creation of vermin harbourage or breeding sites.

sections.section: ## (6) Personnel hygiene and food handling practices
sections.summary: Requires food-plant personnel to maintain high personal cleanliness and appropriate clean clothing/headdress, wash hands as needed, prohibit spitting/eating/tobacco/chewing gum in food areas, take precautions to prevent food contamination, treat and waterproof-cover minor hand injuries with first-aid facilities provided, and keep food-handling gloves clean, sanitary and impermeable except where unsuitable for the task.

sections.section: ## D. Operating Practices and Production Requirements
sections.summary: Sets operating requirements from raw material acceptance and storage (rejecting decomposed/toxic/extraneous materials, protecting stored materials) through inspection, sorting and washing with potable or suitably treated water, to timed preparatory and packaging operations to prevent contamination and deterioration; and requires packaging materials to be stored sanitary and packaging conducted to preclude contamination, while heat processing, cooling, container handling, inspection and finished-product storage/transport must ensure product safety and be supervised, recorded and checked by competent personnel and the official agency where applicable.

Recommends each plant designate one individual (preferably separate from production) responsible for plant cleanliness, supported by a permanent, trained sanitation staff and a sanitation schedule that designates critical areas, equipment and materials for specific attention.

Advises plants to have access to laboratory control of sanitary quality—using recognized standard analytical methods, rejecting foods unfit for human consumption and, where appropriate, checking processes by incubation—with the scope of control varying by product and management needs.

Specifies end-product requirements: products should be free from objectionable matter, contain no pathogenic microorganisms or microbially derived toxins, comply with Codex pesticide and additive requirements, and for products with equilibrium pH > 4.5 processing must destroy all Clostridium botulinum spores unless other product characteristics permanently prevent spore growth.
